signals.title: @karpathy: TLDR the people in these two groups are speaking past each other. It really is simultaneou
signals.content:
TLDR the people in these two groups are speaking past each other. It really is simultaneously the case that OpenAI''s free and I think slightly orphaned (?) "Advanced Voice Mode" will fumble the dumbest questions in your Instagram''s reels and *at the same time*, OpenAI''s highest-tier and paid Codex model will go off for 1 hour to coherently restructure an entire code base, or find and exploit vulnerabilities in computer systems. This part really works and has made dramatic strides because 2 properties: 1) these domains offer explicit reward functions that are verifiable meaning they are easily amenable to reinforcement learning training (e.g. unit tests passed yes or no, in contrast to writing, which is much harder to explicitly judge),  but also 2) they are a lot more valuable in b2b settings, meaning that the biggest fraction of the team is focused on improving them. So here we are.' author: id: '33836629' name: Andrej Karpathy screenName: karpathy profileImageUrl: https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1296667294148382721/9Pr6XrPB_normal.jpg verified: true metrics: likes: 20876 retweets: 2529 replies: 1197 quotes: 667 views: 4494391 bookmarks: 11514 createdAt: Thu Apr 09 20:10:52 +0000 2026 createdAtLocal: 2026-04-09 20:10 createdAtISO: '2026-04-09T20:10:52+00:00' media: [] urls: [] isRetweet: false retweetedBy: null lang: en score: null quotedTweet: id: '2042063369432183238' text: The degree to which you are awed by AI is perfectly correlated with how much you use AI to code. author: screenName: staysaasy name: staysaasy id: '2040572272944324650' text: "Farzapedia, personal wikipedia of Farza, good example following my Wiki LLM\ \ tweet.

I really like this approach to personalization in a number of ways,\ \ compared to "status quo" of an AI that allegedly gets better the more you\ \ use it or something:

1. Explicit. The memory artifact is explicit and navigable\ \ (the wiki), you can see exactly what the AI does and does not know and you can\ \ inspect and manage this artifact, even if you don't do the direct text writing\ \ (the LLM does). The knowledge of you is not implicit and unknown, it's explicit\ \ and viewable.
2. Yours. Your data is yours, on your local computer, it's not\ \ in some particular AI provider's system without the ability to extract it. You're\ \ in control of your information. 
3. File over app. The memory here is a simple\ \ collection of files in universal formats (images, markdown). This means the\ \ data is interoperable: you can use a very large collection of tools/CLIs or\ \ whatever you want over this information because it's just files. The agents\ \ can apply the entire Unix toolkit over them. They can natively read and understand\ \ them. Any kind of data can be imported into files as input, and any kind of\ \ interface can be used to view them as the output. E.g. you can use Obsidian\ \ to view them or vibe code something of your own. Search "File over app" for\ \ an article on this philosophy.
4. BYOAI. You can use whatever AI you want to\ \ "plug into" this information - Claude, Codex, OpenCode, whatever. You can\ \ even think about taking an open source AI and finetuning it on your wiki - in\ \ principle, this AI could "know" you in its weights, not just attend over your\ \ data.

So this approach to personalization puts *you* in full control. The\ \ data is yours. In Universal formats. Explicit and inspectable. Use whatever\ \ AI you want over it, keep the AI companies on their toes! :)

Certainly this\ \ is not the simplest way to get an AI to know you - it does require you to manage\ \ file directories and so on, but agents also make it quite simple and they can\ \ help you a lot. I imagine a number of products might come out to make this all\ \ easier, but imo "agent proficiency" is a CORE SKILL of the 21st century. These\ \ are extremely powerful tools - they speak English and they do all the computer\ \ stuff for you. Try this opportunity to play with one." author: id: '33836629' name: Andrej Karpathy screenName: karpathy profileImageUrl: https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1296667294148382721/9Pr6XrPB_normal.jpg verified: true metrics: likes: 9060 retweets: 824 replies: 475 quotes: 131 views: 1418210 bookmarks: 12851 createdAt: Sat Apr 04 23:28:36 +0000 2026 createdAtLocal: 2026-04-04 23:28 createdAtISO: '2026-04-04T23:28:36+00:00' media: [] urls: [] isRetweet: false retweetedBy: null lang: en score: null quotedTweet: id: '2040563939797504467' text: 'This is Farzapedia.

signals.title: @karpathy: ok: true schema_version: '1' data: id: '2039805659525644595' text: "LLM Knowledge Bases\n\
signals.content:
ok: true schema_version: '1' data: id: '2039805659525644595' text: "LLM Knowledge Bases

Something I'm finding very useful recently: using\ \ LLMs to build personal knowledge bases for various topics of research interest.\ \ In this way, a large fraction of my recent token throughput is going less into\ \ manipulating code, and more into manipulating knowledge (stored as markdown\ \ and images). The latest LLMs are quite good at it. So:

Data ingest:
I index\ \ source documents (articles, papers, repos, datasets, images, etc.) into a raw/\ \ directory, then I use an LLM to incrementally "compile" a wiki, which is just\ \ a collection of .md files in a directory structure. The wiki includes summaries\ \ of all the data in raw/, backlinks, and then it categorizes data into concepts,\ \ writes articles for them, and links them all. To convert web articles into .md\ \ files I like to use the Obsidian Web Clipper extension, and then I also use\ \ a hotkey to download all the related images to local so that my LLM can easily\ \ reference them.

IDE:
I use Obsidian as the IDE "frontend" where I can\ \ view the raw data, the the compiled wiki, and the derived visualizations. Important\ \ to note that the LLM writes and maintains all of the data of the wiki, I rarely\ \ touch it directly. I've played with a few Obsidian plugins to render and view\ \ data in other ways (e.g. Marp for slides).

Q&A:
Where things get interesting\ \ is that once your wiki is big enough (e.g. mine on some recent research is ~100\ \ articles and ~400K words), you can ask your LLM agent all kinds of complex questions\ \ against the wiki, and it will go off, research the answers, etc. I thought I\ \ had to reach for fancy RAG, but the LLM has been pretty good about auto-maintaining\ \ index files and brief summaries of all the documents and it reads all the important\ \ related data fairly easily at this ~small scale.

Output:
Instead of getting\ \ answers in text/terminal, I like to have it render markdown files for me, or\ \ slide shows (Marp format), or matplotlib images, all of which I then view again\ \ in Obsidian. You can imagine many other visual output formats depending on the\ \ query. Often, I end up "filing" the outputs back into the wiki to enhance\ \ it for further queries. So my own explorations and queries always "add up"\ \ in the knowledge base.

Linting:
I've run some LLM "health checks" over\ \ the wiki to e.g. find inconsistent data, impute missing data (with web searchers),\ \ find interesting connections for new article candidates, etc., to incrementally\ \ clean up the wiki and enhance its overall data integrity. The LLMs are quite\ \ good at suggesting further questions to ask and look into.

Extra tools:
\ I find myself developing additional tools to process the data, e.g. I vibe coded\ \ a small and naive search engine over the wiki, which I both use directly (in\ \ a web ui), but more often I want to hand it off to an LLM via CLI as a tool\ \ for larger queries. 

Further explorations:
As the repo grows, the natural\ \ desire is to also think about synthetic data generation + finetuning to have\ \ your LLM "know" the data in its weights instead of just context windows.
\ 
TLDR: raw data from a given number of sources is collected, then compiled by\ \ an LLM into a .md wiki, then operated on by various CLIs by the LLM to do Q&A\ \ and to incrementally enhance the wiki, and all of it viewable in Obsidian. You\ \ rarely ever write or edit the wiki manually, it's the domain of the LLM. I think\ \ there is room here for an incredible new product instead of a hacky collection\ \ of scripts." author: id: '33836629' name: Andrej Karpathy screenName: karpathy profileImageUrl: https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1296667294148382721/9Pr6XrPB_normal.jpg verified: true metrics: likes: 59804 retweets: 7237 replies: 2888 quotes: 2143 views: 21369620 bookmarks: 107160 createdAt: Thu Apr 02 20:42:21 +0000 2026 createdAtLocal: 2026-04-02 20:42 createdAtISO: '2026-04-02T20:42:21+00:00' media: [] urls: [] isRetweet: false retweetedBy: null lang: en score: null id: '2040470801506541998' text: 'Wow, this tweet went very viral!
signals.score_reason: Twitter/X discussion signal; agentic AI relevance; retrieval/RAG relevance; large language model relevance. Final score: 68.
signals.brief_en: Utilizing LLMs to build personal knowledge bases for research topics, leveraging data ingest, IDE, and Q&A capabilities to compile and categorize information.
signals.brief_ar: استخدام النماذج اللغوية الكبيرة لإنشاء قواعد معرفة شخصية لمواضيع البحث، وتحقيق قدرات استقبال البيانات وواجهة التطوير وطرق الأسئلة والإجابة لتحويل وتصنيف المعلومات.

signals.title: @karpathy: You can give this to your agent and it can build you your own LLM wiki and guide you on ho
signals.content:
You can give this to your agent and it can build you your own LLM wiki and guide you on how to use it etc. It''s intentionally kept a little bit abstract/vague because there are so many directions to take this in. And ofc, people can adjust the idea or contribute their own in the Discussion which is cool.' author: id: '33836629' name: Andrej Karpathy screenName: karpathy profileImageUrl: https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1296667294148382721/9Pr6XrPB_normal.jpg verified: true metrics: likes: 26775 retweets: 2826 replies: 1120 quotes: 668 views: 7146421 bookmarks: 46619 createdAt: Sat Apr 04 16:45:23 +0000 2026 createdAtLocal: 2026-04-04 16:45 createdAtISO: '2026-04-04T16:45:23+00:00' media: [] urls: [] isRetweet: false retweetedBy: null lang: en score: null quotedTweet: id: '2039805659525644595' text: "LLM Knowledge Bases

Something I'm finding very useful recently: using\ \ LLMs to build personal knowledge bases for various topics of research interest.\ \ In this way, a large fraction of my recent token throughput is going less\ \ into manipulating code, and more into manipulating knowledge (stored as markdown\ \ and images). The latest LLMs are quite good at it. So:

Data ingest:
I\ \ index source documents (articles, papers, repos, datasets, images, etc.) into\ \ a raw/ directory, then I use an LLM to incrementally "compile" a wiki, which\ \ is just a collection of .md files in a directory structure. The wiki includes\ \ summaries of all the data in raw/, backlinks, and then it categorizes data\ \ into concepts, writes articles for them, and links them all. To convert web\ \ articles into .md files I like to use the Obsidian Web Clipper extension,\ \ and then I also use a hotkey to download all the related images to local so\ \ that my LLM can easily reference them.

I use Obsidian as the IDE\ \ "frontend" where I can view the raw data, the the compiled wiki, and the\ \ derived visualizations. Important to note that the LLM writes and maintains\ \ all of the data of the wiki, I rarely touch it directly. I've played with\ \ a few Obsidian plugins to render and view data in other ways (e.g. Marp for\ \ slides).

Q&A:
Where things get interesting is that once your wiki is big\ \ enough (e.g. mine on some recent research is ~100 articles and ~400K words),\ \ you can ask your LLM agent all kinds of complex questions against the wiki,\ \ and it will go off, research the answers, etc. I thought I had to reach for\ \ fancy RAG, but the LLM has been pretty good about auto-maintaining index files\ \ and brief summaries of all the documents and it reads all the important related\ \ data fairly easily at this ~small scale.

Instead of getting answers\ \ in text/terminal, I like to have it render markdown files for me, or slide\ \ shows (Marp format), or matplotlib images, all of which I then view again\ \ in Obsidian. You can imagine many other visual output formats depending on\ \ the query. Often, I end up "filing" the outputs back into the wiki to enhance\ \ it for further queries. So my own explorations and queries always "add up"\ \ in the knowledge base.

Linting:
I've run some LLM "health checks" over\ \ the wiki to e.g. find inconsistent data, impute missing data (with web searchers),\ \ find interesting connections for new article candidates, etc., to incrementally\ \ clean up the wiki and enhance its overall data integrity. The LLMs are quite\ \ good at suggesting further questions to ask and look into.

Extra tools:
\ I find myself developing additional tools to process the data, e.g. I vibe coded\ \ a small and naive search engine over the wiki, which I both use directly (in\ \ a web ui), but more often I want to hand it off to an LLM via CLI as a tool\ \ for larger queries. 

Further explorations:
As the repo grows, the natural\ \ desire is to also think about synthetic data generation + finetuning to have\ \ your LLM "know" the data in its weights instead of just context windows.
\ 
TLDR: raw data from a given number of sources is collected, then compiled\ \ by an LLM into a .md wiki, then operated on by various CLIs by the LLM to\ \ do Q&A and to incrementally enhance the wiki, and all of it viewable in Obsidian.\ \ You rarely ever write or edit the wiki manually, it's the domain of the LLM.\ \ I think there is room here for an incredible new product instead of a hacky\ \ collection of scripts." author: screenName: karpathy name: Andrej Karpathy id: '2064409694761054332' text: 'This is a super exciting release - Claude Fable 5 is the same underlying model as Mythos but with added safeguards. The benchmarks are great and it''s SOTA on everything by a margin but I''ll add that *qualitatively* also, this is a major-version-bump-deserving step change forward (imo of the same order as Claude 4.5 was in November), peaking especially for long problem-solving sessions on very difficult problems. You can give it a lot more ambitious tasks than what you''re used to, the model "gets it" and it will just go, and it''s never felt this tempting to stop looking at the code at all (but don''t do this in prod!). The model still has quirks that people will run into and the safeguards are configured to be a little too trigger happy for launch, which can hopefully be tuned over time.

signals.brief_en: Andrej Karpathy suggests using LLMs to build personal knowledge bases, indexing source documents and using LLMs to compile a wiki, which can be used to guide and provide information.
signals.brief_ar: يقترح Andrej Karpathy استخدام LLMs لبناء قواعد معرفة شخصية، وفهرسة الوثائق المصدرية و使用 LLMs لتحويلها إلى ويكي، والتي يمكن استخدامها لتقديم إرشاد ومعلومات.

signals.title: Evaluating Open-Source LLMs for Multi-Label ATT&CK Technique Classification on CTI Reports
signals.content: Classifying Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) using MITRE Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge (ATT&CK) is essential for proactive defense, but historically required extensive human effort. Pre-Large Language Model (LLM) automation sped up this process, but could not resolve the complex language and multi-step attack patterns found in unstructured CTI reports. LLMs addressed previous limitations by using contextual reasoning to understand unstructured text. However, current evaluations rely on simplified, single-technique sentences that ignore the complexity of real-world CTI reports, which often leads to inflated performance results. Consequently, the baseline performance of open-source LLMs on complex unstructured CTI reports remains unevaluated. To address this gap, we constructed a ground-truth dataset of 2,076 human-annotated sentences (1,281 technique-positive, 795 negative) from 83 complex unstructured CTI reports. These sentences were mapped to 114 unique ATT&CK techniques using a six-phase annotation process, achieving \k{appa} = 0.68 inter-annotator agreement. Using this dataset, we evaluated seven open-source LLMs ranging from 8B to 236B parameters across prompt strategy and temperature configurations. The highest-performing LLM achieved a micro-averaged F1 score of 0.22, establishing the empirical baseline for multi-label ATT&CK classification on complex unstructured CTI. Parameter size showed a statistically significant positive correlation with F1 score. Prompt strategy and temperature produced no statistically significant gains across model configurations. These results indicate that current open-source LLMs are insufficient for production-grade ATT&CK classification. The dataset, benchmark, and findings provide a reproducible foundation for future CTI research.
signals.score_reason: academic/research signal; retrieval/RAG relevance; large language model relevance; model capability relevance. Final score: 67.
signals.brief_en: Evaluating Open-Source LLMs for Multi-Label ATT&CK Technique Classification on CTI Reports: A study assessing the performance of open-source Large Language Models (LLMs) in classifying Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports using MITRE ATT&CK framework, highlighting the challenges and limitations of current evaluations and establishing a baseline performance for multi-label ATT&CK classification on complex unstructured CTI reports.
signals.brief_ar: تقييم النماذج اللغوية الكبيرة المفتوحة المصدر لتصنيف تقنيات ATT&CK متعددة التعليمة على تقارير المخابرات الإلكترونية: دراسة لتقييم أداء النماذج اللغوية الكبيرة المفتوحة المصدر في تصنيف تقارير المخابرات الإلكترونية باستخدام إطار عمل MITRE ATT&CK، وتسليط الضوء على التحديات والقيود الحالية للتقييمات ووضع معيار أداء لتصنيف ATT&CK متعددة التعليمة على تقارير المخابرات الإلكترونية غير المنظمة المعقدة

signals.title: @karpathy: I wanted share a possibly slightly improved version of the tweet in an "idea file". The id
signals.content: I wanted share a possibly slightly improved version of the tweet in an "idea file". The idea of the idea file is that in this era of LLM agents, there is less of a point/need of sharing the specific code/app, you just share the idea, then the other person''s agent customizes & builds it for your specific needs.
signals.score_reason: Twitter/X discussion signal; agentic AI relevance; large language model relevance. Final score: 66.
signals.brief_en: AI expert @karpathy suggests sharing ideas instead of code, leveraging LLM agents to customize and build applications based on individual needs.

signals.title: Learning Cardiac Electrophysiology Digital Twins Through Agentic Discovery of Hybrid Structure
signals.content: Building personalized cardiac electrophysiology (EP) digital twins requires identifying the appropriate model structure for each patient, not merely fitting parameters. Traditional methods rely on experts to manually prescribe hybrid physics-neural architectures, which requires deep domain expertise and does not transfer across patients. Recent works have applied large language models (LLMs) to generate or act as hybrid models. However, despite their promising generalization capacity, these LLM-based methods lack the structural priors needed for stable cardiac simulations. Hence, we propose LEADS, a framework that formulates cardiac EP domain knowledge as a structured action space and utilizes an LLM agent to discover hybrid models. The agent follows an iterative reasoning-and-action loop to select, combine, and refine hybrid models, whilst gradient descent handles parameter fitting. The proposed LEADS designs every candidate model towards physically grounded, interpretable, and numerically stable, while allowing open-ended architectural discovery. We validate LEADS on synthetic data with three ground-truth reaction models and on real cardiac EP data, demonstrating that it outperforms both human-designed hybrid models and other LLM-based hybrid modeling.
signals.score_reason: academic/research signal; agentic AI relevance; large language model relevance; model capability relevance. Final score: 54.
signals.brief_en: LEADS: A framework for discovering hybrid cardiac electrophysiology digital twins using large language models, outperforming human-designed and other LLM-based models.
signals.brief_ar: LEADS: إطار لاكتشاف النمذجة الهجينة للتوائم الرقمية للفيزيولوجيا الكهربائية القلبية باستخدام نماذج اللغة الكبيرة، وتفوق النماذج المصممة من قبل البشر والنمذجة الهجينة الأخرى القائمة على نماذج اللغة الكبيرة.

signals.title: The Measurement Gap in the Automation of EU Law: Benchmarking Doctrinal Legal Reasoning under the EU AI Act
signals.content: Large language models now produce legal text of at least median quality, yet no existing benchmark can evaluate whether they perform doctrinal legal reasoning, which forms the interpretive core of legal work, rather than the ancillary, paralegal tasks that most current legal-AI evaluations measure. This measurement gap is not only methodological but legal: the EU AI Act makes "appropriate accuracy" a binding requirement for high-risk AI used in the judicial domain, yet that requirement cannot acquire operational content without the very doctrinal-reasoning benchmark the field lacks.
signals.score_reason: academic/research signal; model capability relevance. Final score: 48.
signals.brief_en: The EU AI Act requires "appropriate accuracy" for high-risk AI in the judicial domain, but a measurement gap exists in evaluating doctrinal legal reasoning, hindering the assessment of large language models' performance.
signals.brief_ar: يتطلب قانون الاتحاد الأوروبي للذكاء الاصطناعي "دقة مناسبة" للذكاء الاصطناعي عالي الخطورة في المجال القضائي، لكن هناك فجوة في القياس لتقييم الاستدلال القانوني الدوغمائي، مما يعوق تقييم أداء نماذج اللغة الكبيرة.

signals.title: @karpathy: TLDR The input/output mind meld between humans and AIs is ongoing and there is a lot of wo
signals.content: TLDR The input/output mind meld between humans and AIs is ongoing and there is a lot of work to do and significant progress to be made, way before jumping all the way into neuralink-esque BCIs and all that. For what''s worth exploring at the current stage, hot tip try ask for HTML.' author: id: '33836629' name: Andrej Karpathy screenName: karpathy profileImageUrl: https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1296667294148382721/9Pr6XrPB_normal.jpg verified: true metrics: likes: 19293 retweets: 2020 replies: 1040 quotes: 468 views: 3840612 bookmarks: 21508 createdAt: Mon May 11 16:20:21 +0000 2026 createdAtLocal: 2026-05-11 16:20 createdAtISO: '2026-05-11T16:20:21+00:00' media: [] urls: [] isRetweet: false retweetedBy: null lang: en score: null quotedTweet: id: '2052809885763747935' text: https://t.co/MXt5XS4xBX author: screenName: trq212 name: Thariq id: '2041162213160091996' text: The core idea is that this lets you skip writing but it doesn’t let you skip reading and thinking. And the surprising result is that this works. signals.title: IUU+DB: Tracking Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated Fishing, Seafood Fraud, and Labor Abuse through LLM-driven Information Extraction
signals.content: Illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing (IUU) traditionally refers to fishing activities that violate applicable laws or occur in areas that lack applicable laws. We propose the term IUU+ to capture a broader suite of fisheries sector environmental and associated supply chain trade-related crimes and behaviors. Although IUU+ activity is widely recognized as a serious threat to marine ecosystems, markets, and livelihoods, a quantitative understanding of these incidents, e.g., their frequency, geography, species, actors, and patterns in the type of illicit activity, remains difficult to obtain. We propose IUU+DB, a large language model driven system for building a global incident database of IUU+ activity. The system ingests heterogeneous documents, classifies whether they describe relevant incidents, extracts key data elements such as actors, locations, species, vessels, violations, and enforcement outcomes, and supports deduplication and trend analysis. Case studies and validation results show that IUU+DB can help organize fragmented evidence, surface geographic and behavioral hotspots, support fisheries-domain specific research in academia and non-government organizations, assist source and species risk assessments for industry, and provide support for policy implementation and targeted enforcement efforts to government agencies.
signals.score_reason: academic/research signal; retrieval/RAG relevance; large language model relevance; model capability relevance. Final score: 47.
signals.brief_en: IUU+DB: A large language model-driven system for tracking illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing, seafood fraud, and labor abuse through information extraction.
